perturbation.original_text: (8) Term . The Term of this Agreement commences on the date first set forth above and extends for a period of three (3) years thereafter unless otherwise agreed upon in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: (8) Term. The Term of this Agreement commences on the date first set forth above and extends perpetually.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the confidentiality term to 'perpetually' may be problematic under Massachusetts law, which generally disfavors perpetual restrictions, especially in business contexts. Non-compete agreements and overly broad confidentiality clauses can be challenged as unreasonable restraints on trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, Section 2 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. While this law doesn't directly prohibit perpetual confidentiality agreements, a court might find that such an agreement is an unfair business practice because it unreasonably restricts the use of information and potentially stifles competition, especially if the information loses its competitive value over time. The modification to 'perpetually' introduces a term that could be challenged under this statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the contract term to extend 'perpetually,' which introduces a potential legal contradiction under Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93A, Section 2. This law, as evidenced in both scraped snippets, prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts in trade or commerce. While the law does not explicitly ban perpetual confidentiality agreements, the LLM explanation suggests that a court might view such an agreement as an unfair business practice because it unreasonably restricts the use of information and could stifle competition, especially if the information loses its value over time. The 'perpetually' term could be challenged as an unreasonable restraint on trade, thus contradicting the intent of MGL c. 93A, § 2 to prevent unfair competition.

perturbation.original_text: (5) Termination of Discussions . Following termination of discussions regarding a Possible Transaction, upon the request (which request shall be made within a reasonable time after such termination of discussions) of the Disclosing Party for any reason, Recipient will, at Recipient’s option, either deliver to the Disclosing Party or destroy (which destruction shall be certified in writing by an officer of Recipient) all Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party pursuant hereto and the portion of all other Confidential Information prepared by Recipient or its Representatives which contains Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party
perturbation.changed_text: (5) Termination of Discussions . Following termination of discussions regarding a Possible Transaction, upon the request of the Disclosing Party for any reason, Recipient will, at Recipient’s sole discretion, maintain all Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party pursuant hereto and the portion of all other Confidential Information prepared by Recipient or its Representatives which contains Confidential Information furnished to Recipient or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party
perturbation.explanation: By changing the requirement to return or destroy confidential information to allowing the recipient to maintain it at their sole discretion, the modified text creates a potential violation of trade secret laws if the information qualifies as a trade secret under Massachusetts law. The original text provided a process to terminate the confidentiality obligations but maintaining the information indefinitely could lead to misappropriation claims if the information is used improperly. Note that this is a highly specific set of facts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93, Section 42 defines 'trade secret'. If the 'Confidential Information' qualifies as a trade secret under this definition, the modified text could enable future misappropriation. While the original text aimed to protect confidential information through return/destruction, the modified text introduces an element of keeping such trade secrets beyond the termination, contradicting the protections provided by this Massachusetts statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Massachusetts trade secret law. The original text required the recipient to either return or destroy confidential information, thus providing a mechanism to terminate confidentiality obligations. The changed text allows the recipient to 'maintain' the information at their sole discretion. If the confidential information qualifies as a trade secret under Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93, Section 42, this change introduces the potential for misappropriation. As defined in Scraped Law Snippet 1, 'Misappropriation' includes 'an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent'. By allowing the recipient to maintain the information indefinitely, the modified contract creates a scenario where the recipient could potentially use or disclose the trade secret without consent, which directly contradicts the law's protection against misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: (11) Governing Law . This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts applicable to agreements made and to be performed entirely within Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: (11) Governing Law . This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Massachusetts to the People's Republic of China could substantially alter the enforceability and interpretation of the contract. Massachusetts has well-established contract law, whereas Chinese law may offer different protections and remedies, especially concerning intellectual property and data privacy. This could disadvantage one party significantly depending on the nature of the dispute and the specifics of Chinese law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 106, Section 1-301, allows parties to agree on the governing law, but the choice must bear a reasonable relation to the transaction. If the transaction has no substantial connection to China, a Massachusetts court may refuse to enforce the choice of Chinese law on public policy grounds. Also, M.G.L. c. 93A §2, preventing unfair methods of competition, could potentially come into play if using Chinese law is a strategy to be deceptive. The change contradicts Massachusetts' established principles for choice of law in contracts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article1, Section 1-301 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Section 1-301 Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1-301 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1-301: Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power To Choose Applicable Law Section 1–301. (a) Except as provided hereafter in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to the commonwealth and also to another state or nation, the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction. Failing to reach such an agreement, this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to the commonwealth. (b) To the extent that this chapter governs a transaction, if one of the following sections specifies the applicable law, that section governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the section specified: (1) section 2–402; (2) sections 2A–105 and 2A–106; (3) section 4–102; (4) section 4A–507; (5) section 5–116; (6) section 8–110; and (7) sections 9–301 through 9–307. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... (a) Except as provided hereafter in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to the commonwealth and also to another state or nation, the parties may agree that the law of either the commonwealth or of such other state or nation shall govern the rights and duties under the transaction. Failing to reach such an agreement, this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to the commonwealth. (b) To the extent that this chapter governs a transaction, if one of the following sections specifies the applicable law, that section governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the section specified: (1) section 2–402; (2) sections 2A–105 and 2A–106; (3) section 4–102; (4) section 4A–507; (5) section 5–116; (6) section 8–110; and (7) sections 9–301 through 9–307. perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts Massachusetts General Law Chapter 106, Section 1-301, regarding the choice of governing law. The original text specifies Massachusetts law, while the changed text opts for the law of the People's Republic of China. Section 1-301(a) states that when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to the commonwealth and another state or nation, the parties may agree on the governing law of either. However, if the transaction lacks a 'reasonable relation' to China, a Massachusetts court might not enforce the choice of Chinese law. The altered text makes the contract contradict the law, as the contract now may violate established principles for choice of law. The scraped snippets support this, directly quoting Section 1-301(a) in both snippets.

perturbation.original_text: (4) Required Disclosure . In the event that Recipient or any of its Representatives are requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information, Recipient shall provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice of any such request or requirement so that the Disclosing Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (4) Required Disclosure . In the event that Recipient or any of its Representatives are requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the Confidential Information or Discussion Information, Recipient shall immediately disclose such information without notifying the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires prompt notification to the Disclosing Party, allowing them to seek a protective order. By removing this notification requirement and mandating immediate disclosure, the modified text directly contradicts established legal principles regarding the protection of trade secrets and confidential information during litigation. The original agreement provided a mechanism to protect confidentiality, but that is lost.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93, Section 42 defines "trade secret". Immediate disclosure without notice eliminates the opportunity to seek a protective order, potentially leading to the unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets. The original text aimed to mitigate this risk, but the modified version removes such protections, leading to contravention of the principles underlying trade secret protection.

As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the intention of Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93, Section 42 regarding the protection of trade secrets. By removing the requirement to notify the Disclosing Party before disclosing confidential information, the Recipient is no longer obligated to allow the Disclosing Party the opportunity to seek a protective order. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the disclosure of a trade secret without express or implied consent, particularly when the knowledge was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use. The original text allowed for such limitation through a protective order; the changed text removes this possibility, increasing the risk of misappropriation by unauthorized disclosure. Therefore, the contradiction exists because the modification undermines the legal principles aimed at preventing the unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets.
